title: Paper clay seashell wall arrangement
subtitle: Six to eight sculpted shell forms for wall display
excerpt: A collection of six to eight paper clay seashell forms including conch spirals, ridged clams, and scallop discs, each with a pierced hanging hole or magnet embed. No kiln needed. PVA sealed and displayed as a wall grouping.

glossaryTerms.definition: air-dry clay with cellulose fibres added, which bridge cracks and strengthen thin sections
the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
clay diluted with water to a brushable or pourable consistency
a joining technique where both surfaces are scratched (scored) and coated with wet clay (slip) before pressing together to create a secure bond

content.text: This arrangement uses
paper clay

to build six to eight shell forms across three types: a spiral conch built from a coil, a ridged clam pressed directly over a real shell, and a flat disc scallop with incised ribs. Paper clay holds the fine ridge detail of a real shell without cracking at the thin trailing edges, which plain air-dry clay cannot reliably do. Each piece has a pierced hanging hole or an embedded small rare-earth magnet for flush wall mounting. No kiln is required. Finished pieces are PVA sealed and can be left natural white or given a dilute watercolour wash for coastal tones.
Preparation
If making ridged clam impressions, collect two or three actual shells with clearly defined ridges. Wash and dry them. Make a small cup of
slip
from paper clay scraps dissolved in water to a thick cream consistency for
score-and-slip

joins. Decide before building whether each shell will hang from a cord (pierced hole) or mount flush to a wall (magnet embed). For magnet mounting, have small disc rare-earth magnets ready to embed while the clay is soft. Build the three shell types in batches : complete all conch spirals first, then clams, then scallops.
Construction

Spiral conch: roll a coil 200 mm long that tapers from 15 mm diameter at one end to a point at the other. Flatten the wide end slightly with your thumb. Coil the tapered end around itself in a spiral, working from the pointed tip inward, until the whole coil forms a rough cone shape 70 mm tall and 50 mm at the base. Press each coil layer against the one below to fuse them. Blend the outer surface with a damp sponge. Use the needle tool to incise four or five curved spiral grooves over the outside surface, following the direction of the coil.

Ridged clam: roll a slab 7 mm thick. Press the outside face of a real clam shell firmly into the clay surface : use the outside of the shell for an outward-relief impression. Cut around the shell outline with the fettling knife, leaving a 5 mm border. Lift the clay oval free from the board and curve it gently in your hands to give it a slight bowl shape : real clams are not flat. Set the shaped piece convex side up on the board to maintain the curve as it dries.

Flat disc scallop: roll a slab 6 mm thick. Cut a fan shape with the fettling knife: the flat edge at the top (the hinge end) about 60 mm wide, and the curved outer edge making a half-circle below it. Use the needle tool to incise 10 to 12 radiating ribs from the hinge point outward to the curved edge, fanning evenly across the surface. Create a shallow dimple at the hinge point with the blunt end of the needle tool.

Repeat to make two or three of each shell type, varying sizes: a 70 mm conch, a 50 mm conch, a large clam and a small clam, a 70 mm scallop and a 50 mm scallop. Variation in scale gives the wall arrangement visual depth.
Hanging hole method: once each shell is at
leather-hard
stage, pierce a 4 mm hole at the top, 8 mm from the edge. Rotate the needle tool gently. Smooth the hole rim. For the spiral conch, pierce through the back of the base coil.

Magnet embed method: while the clay is still soft (not yet leather-hard), press a small disc magnet into the reverse face of each shell piece, centred, so the magnet sits flush with the back surface. Press the clay firmly around the magnet edge to lock it in place. Leave undisturbed until past leather-hard.
Drying

Set all pieces on the wooden board with their display face up. Conch spirals can rest on their flat base. Curved clam pieces need a slight prop to maintain the curve: rest them on a folded cloth or rolled paper. Leave for 48 to 3 days until
bone-dry

. Paper clay's cellulose fibres prevent most cracking at thin shell edges, but check the tips of the scallop fan and the pointed apex of the conch spiral after 24 hours. Apply a small brush of paper clay slip to any forming hairline cracks before they open fully.
Finishing

Seal all surfaces with two coats of undiluted PVA. For a coastal watercolour effect: before PVA sealing, brush very dilute watercolour (blue-grey, sandy ochre, or shell pink) over each piece with the mop brush and wipe back lightly with a damp cloth so colour concentrates in the texture grooves. Allow the watercolour to dry completely before PVA sealing. For magnet-mounted shells, ensure no PVA enters the magnet cavity : wipe the magnet face clean of any PVA before it dries.
Care

Display indoors. Dust with a soft brush. The individual pieces are light enough for cord hanging or magnet mounting without placing stress on the wall surface. Re-seal with PVA if the surface develops powdery patches over time.
Variations
Mount all pieces on a single backing board (driftwood or painted plywood) using strong adhesive. This creates a framed coastal display instead of individual hanging.
Mix shell forms with pressed botanical impressions on flat tile panels. Hang both types together in the same grouping, using the botanical wall panel pattern for the tile panels.
Make a set of 10 miniature shells at 30 to 40 mm each and string together with fine cord for a wall garland.

Incise a fine grid texture over the back of each shell before it reaches bone-dry, then seal. The textured back can face forward on a flat shelf instead of hanging on the wall. This gives a second display option.

rows.problem: Conch coil layers separate as they dry
rows.cause: Layers not fused firmly enough while the clay was soft
rows.fix: While still soft, press each coil layer firmly against the one below with a damp fingertip and blend the join line. Apply paper clay slip to any opening gaps at leather-hard.

rows.problem: Clam impression too faint : ridges barely visible
rows.cause: Shell pressed into clay with insufficient force or slab too thick
rows.fix: Roll the slab to 7 mm and press the shell firmly with both hands, applying even pressure across the full surface. Deepen faint ridges with the needle tool after peeling.

rows.problem: Scallop rib lines crack and lift on drying
rows.cause: Incised lines cut too deep relative to the slab thickness
rows.fix: Keep rib incisions to 2 to 3 mm depth on a 6 mm slab. Paper clay bridges micro-cracks that do appear : apply paper clay slip to any crack that opens before bone-dry.

rows.cause: Magnet not pressed deeply enough or clay not sealed around its edges
rows.fix: Re-embed while clay is still soft: push the magnet in so its top sits 1 mm below the surface and press the clay firmly all around the edge. If already dry, bed the magnet in a pool of PVA and allow to cure overnight.

rows.problem: Watercolour wash looks blotchy after sealing
rows.cause: Watercolour not fully dry before PVA applied, or applied too unevenly
rows.fix: Let watercolour dry fully, at least two hours. Apply PVA in thin strokes and avoid going back over already-wet PVA strokes, which disturb the colour layer beneath.
